fa: First king to unite the Franks, converted to Christianity at Reims
Reunified the Frankish kingdom after years of division
Brutal ruler known for conflicts with his wife Frédégonde
Last effective Merovingian king, made Paris his capital
Last Merovingian king, deposed by Pépin le Bref
First Carolingian king, father of Charlemagne, allied with the Pope
Crowned Holy Roman Emperor in 800, unified much of Western Europe
Son of Charlemagne, attempted to maintain the unified empire
First King of West Francia after the Treaty of Verdun (843)
Short reign, son of Charles the Bald
Defeated the Vikings at the Battle of Saucourt (881)
Co-ruled with his brother Louis III
Briefly reunited the Carolingian empire before deposition
Defended Paris against Vikings, first non-Carolingian king
Ceded Normandy to Viking chief Rollo in 911
Elected king by rebellious nobles, killed in battle
Defended against Magyar and Viking invasions
Raised in England, struggled to reclaim royal authority
Last powerful Carolingian king of West Francia
Last Carolingian king, died without heir
Founded the Capetian dynasty, which ruled France for 341 years
Known for his piety, excommunicated by the Pope
Allied with William the Conqueror before their falling out
First French king to be excommunicated for adultery
Consolidated royal power and fought feudal lords
Led the Second Crusade, divorced Eleanor of Aquitaine
Doubled French territory, defeated England at Bouvines (1214)
Invaded England, led the Albigensian Crusade
Led two crusades, canonized in 1297, model of Christian kingship
Expanded French territory through diplomacy and inheritance
Suppressed the Knights Templar, moved the papacy to Avignon
First king to free serfs in France
Established the Salic Law excluding women from succession
Last direct Capetian king, died without male heir
First Valois king, defeated at Crécy (1346) during the Hundred Years War
Captured by the English at Poitiers (1356), held for ransom
Rebuilt France after English defeats, reorganized the army
Suffered from mental illness, signed Treaty of Troyes ceding France to England
Aided by Joan of Arc, expelled the English and ended Hundred Years War
Masterful politician nicknamed 'the Universal Spider', unified France
Invaded Italy in 1494, starting the Italian Wars
Reduced taxes, reformed justice, continued Italian campaigns
Renaissance patron, invited Leonardo da Vinci, built Château de Chambord
Persecuted Protestants, died in a jousting accident
Brief reign at 15, husband of Mary Queen of Scots
Ordered the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre (1572)
Last Valois king, assassinated by a monk
Ended Wars of Religion, issued Edict of Nantes, assassinated by Ravaillac
Relied on Cardinal Richelieu, strengthened absolute monarchy
Longest reign in European history (72 yrs), built Versailles, peak of French power
Lost Canada and India to Britain, laid groundwork for Revolution
Last king before the Revolution, executed by guillotine on 21 January 1793
Restored after Napoleon, established constitutional monarchy
Absolutist policies provoked the July Revolution, last Bourbon king to reign
Last king of France, overthrown by 1848 Revolution
